infobox.value: | [The Crown](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown")  * [Monarch](/wiki/Monarchy_of_New_Zealand "Monarchy of New Zealand") [King Charles III](/wiki/Charles_III "Charles III") * [Governor-General](/wiki/Governor-General_of_New_Zealand "Governor-General of New Zealand") ([list](/wiki/List_of_governors-general_of_New_Zealand "List of governors-general of New Zealand")) [Cindy Kiro](/wiki/Cindy_Kiro "Cindy Kiro") * [Realm of New Zealand](/wiki/Realm_of_New_Zealand "Realm of New Zealand")

are the administrative jurisdictions of the country's **regional councils** and [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"); the country is divided into sixteen such areas. The councils derive their powers from the [central government](/wiki/New_Zealand_Government "New Zealand Government"), as [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") rather than a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation").

The **regions of New Zealand** are the administrative jurisdictions of the country's **regional councils** and [unitary authorities](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority"); the country is divided into sixteen such areas. The councils derive their powers from the [central government](/wiki/New_Zealand_Government "New Zealand Government"), as [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand") is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") rather than a [federation](/wiki/Federation "Federation").

Eleven are currently administered by regional councils, whilst the other five are administered by unitary authorities.[[a]](#cite_note-1) Most of [New Zealand's outlying Islands](/wiki/New_Zealand_outlying_islands "New Zealand outlying islands") are not included within its regions, with the [Solander Islands](/wiki/Solander_Islands "Solander Islands") being the exception as they are within the [Southland Region](/wiki/Southland_Region "Southland Region"). The [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") are not within any region, having [their own specially legislated territorial authority](/wiki/Chatham_Islands_Council "Chatham Islands Council").

## Current regions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Current regions")]

### History and statutory basis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History and statutory basis")]

The regional councils are listed in Part 1 of Schedule 2 of the [Local Government Act 2002](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_2002 "Local Government Act 2002"),[[1]](#cite_note-2) along with reference to the *[Gazette](/wiki/New_Zealand_Gazette "New Zealand Gazette")* notices that established them in 1989.[[2]](#cite_note-3) The act requires regional councils to promote [sustainable development](/wiki/Sustainable_development "Sustainable development") – the social, economic, environmental and cultural well-being of their communities.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

The current regions and most of their councils came into being through a [local government reform in 1989](/wiki/1989_New_Zealand_local_government_reforms "1989 New Zealand local government reforms") that took place under the [Local Government Act 1974](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1974_(New_Zealand) "Local Government Act 1974 (New Zealand)"). The regional councils replaced the more than 700 ad hoc bodies that had been formed in the preceding century – roads boards, catchment boards, drainage boards, pest control boards, harbour boards, domain and reserve boards.[[4]](#cite_note-bush-5) In addition they took over some roles that had previously been performed by county councils.

The boundaries of the regions are based largely on [drainage basins](/wiki/Drainage_basin "Drainage basin").[[5]](#cite_note-OECD-6) This anticipated the responsibilities of the [Resource Management Act 1991](/wiki/Resource_Management_Act_1991 "Resource Management Act 1991").[[6]](#cite_note-NZHA98-7) Most regional boundaries conform with territorial authority boundaries but there are a number of exceptions. An example is [Taupō District](/wiki/Taup%C5%8D_District "Taupō District"), split between four regions, although most of its area is in the [Waikato](/wiki/Waikato "Waikato") region.[[7]](#cite_note-8) There is often a high degree of co-operation between regional and territorial councils as they have complementary roles.

### Resource management functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Resource management functions")]

Regional councils have these specific functions under the [Resource Management Act 1991](/wiki/Resource_Management_Act_1991 "Resource Management Act 1991"):

* Planning for the integrated management of natural and physical resources[[8]](#cite_note-9)
* Planning for regionally significant land uses[[9]](#cite_note-10)
* [Soil conservation](/wiki/Soil_conservation "Soil conservation"), water quality and quantity, water ecosystems, natural hazards, hazardous substances [[10]](#cite_note-11)
* Controlling the coastal marine area[[11]](#cite_note-12)
* Controlling via [resource consents](/wiki/Resource_consent "Resource consent") the taking, use, damming or diverting of water [[12]](#cite_note-13)
* Controlling via resource consents the discharge of contaminants[[13]](#cite_note-14)
* Establishing of rules in a regional plan to allocate water[[14]](#cite_note-15)
* Controlling via resource consents the beds of waterbodies[[15]](#cite_note-16)

### Other functions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Other functions")]

Regional councils have responsibility for functions under other statutes;[[16]](#cite_note-17)

* flood and river control under the Soil Conservation and Rivers Control Act 1941,
* reserves vested in regional councils under the [Reserves Act 1977](/wiki/Reserves_Act_1977 "Reserves Act 1977"),
* civil defence under the Civil Defence Act 1990,
* regional pest management under the [Biosecurity Act 1993](/wiki/Biosecurity_Act_1993 "Biosecurity Act 1993"),
* harbour and water navigation under the [Maritime Transport Act 1994](/wiki/Maritime_Transport_Act_1994 "Maritime Transport Act 1994"),
* [hazardous waste](/wiki/Hazardous_waste "Hazardous waste") under the HSNO Act 1996,
* public transport planning under the Land Transport Act 1998, and
* supervision of the safety of dams under the Building Act 2004.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

### Proposed abolition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Proposed abolition")]

In mid 2025, [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_New_Zealand "Prime Minister of New Zealand") [Christopher Luxon](/wiki/Christopher_Luxon "Christopher Luxon") and Regional Development Minister [Shane Jones](/wiki/Shane_Jones "Shane Jones") proposed scrapping the 11 regional councils as part of the [Sixth National Government](/wiki/Sixth_National_Government_of_New_Zealand "Sixth National Government of New Zealand")'s proposed overhaul of the [Resource Management Act 1991](/wiki/Resource_Management_Act_1991 "Resource Management Act 1991").[[18]](#cite_note-19) In late November 2025, [Local Government Minister](/wiki/Minister_of_Local_Government_(New_Zealand) "Minister of Local Government (New Zealand)") [Simon Watts](/wiki/Simon_Watts "Simon Watts") and RMA Reform Minister [Chris Bishop](/wiki/Chris_Bishop "Chris Bishop") confirmed the Government would seek to abolish regional councils and transfer their responsibilities to the 67 [local district and city councils](/wiki/Territorial_authorities_of_New_Zealand "Territorial authorities of New Zealand").[[19]](#cite_note-Palmer_25_Nov_2025-20)[[20]](#cite_note-Coughlan_25_Nov_2025-21)

The Government has released two replacement proposals. First, abolishing elected regional councillors and replacing them with combined territories boards, which would consist of the mayors of the district councils within the former regions. While the regional councils as organisations would remain, they would be run by the mayors of the constituent regions. The Government's second proposal would be to get the combined territories boards to prepare a regional reorganisation plan within two years of their establishment, subject to approval by the Local Government Minister. These plans would focus on the delivery of infrastructure, public services and regulatory functions, and would be tested against criteria based on housing, infrastructure, and manageable rates services.[[20]](#cite_note-Coughlan_25_Nov_2025-21) Public consultation on the proposed legislative changes was open until 20 February 2026. The legislation is expected to be introduced in mid-2026 with the goal of passing it into law by 2027.[[19]](#cite_note-Palmer_25_Nov_2025-20)

The Government's proposal to abolish the regional councils attracted a mixture of reactions. The proposal was supported by [Otago Regional councillor](/wiki/Otago_Regional_Council "Otago Regional Council") [Michael Laws](/wiki/Michael_Laws "Michael Laws") and [Dunedin City councillor](/wiki/Dunedin_City_Council "Dunedin City Council") [Lee Vandervis](/wiki/Lee_Vandervis "Lee Vandervis"), who described the regional councils as an "excessive level" of bureaucracy and argued that their dissolution would lower [rates](/wiki/Rate_(tax) "Rate (tax)") and [red tape](/wiki/Red_tape "Red tape").[[21]](#cite_note-Hepburn_26_Nov_2025-22) By contrast, the proposal was opposed by [Mayor of Wellington](/wiki/Mayor_of_Wellington "Mayor of Wellington") [Andrew Little](/wiki/Andrew_Little_(New_Zealand_politician) "Andrew Little (New Zealand politician)"), Otago Regional councillor Alan Somerville, [Te Wānanga o Raukawa](/wiki/Te_W%C4%81nanga_o_Raukawa "Te Wānanga o Raukawa") environmental planning lecturer Mahina-a-rangi Baker, Te Rūnanga o Makaawhio chair Paul Madgwick and [Mayor of Dunedin](/wiki/Mayor_of_Dunedin "Mayor of Dunedin") [Sophie Barker](/wiki/Sophie_Barker_(mayor) "Sophie Barker (mayor)") on the grounds that abolishing the regional councils would erode environmental protections, reduce Māori input in regional-level decision-making, infringe on [Treaty of Waitangi](/wiki/Treaty_of_Waitangi "Treaty of Waitangi") obligations and add further to mayors and territorial authorities' workloads.[[21]](#cite_note-Hepburn_26_Nov_2025-22)[[22]](#cite_note-23)[[23]](#cite_note-RNZ_26_Nov_2025-24)[[24]](#cite_note-25)[[25]](#cite_note-26) Meanwhile, [Mayor of Central Otago](/wiki/Mayor_of_Central_Otago "Mayor of Central Otago") [Tamah Alley](/wiki/Tamah_Alley "Tamah Alley"), [Mayor of Gisborne](/wiki/Mayor_of_Gisborne "Mayor of Gisborne") [Rehette Stoltz](/wiki/Rehette_Stoltz "Rehette Stoltz") and [Labour](/wiki/New_Zealand_Labour_Party "New Zealand Labour Party") leader [Chris Hipkins](/wiki/Chris_Hipkins "Chris Hipkins") expressed concerns about accountability, the loss of technical expertise, and the nature of the proposed reforms.[[21]](#cite_note-Hepburn_26_Nov_2025-22)[[23]](#cite_note-RNZ_26_Nov_2025-24)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

In mid-February 2026, several Otago regional councillors criticised the Government's proposal to replace the regional councils. Michael Laws described the proposal as "poorly written, drafted by bureaucrats who used AI when it was in a hallucination state and drawn up by people who had not passed their NCEA literacy tests." Instead, Laws advocated maintaining a regional government in Otago and a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority "Unitary authority") based on the [Auckland Council](/wiki/Auckland_Council "Auckland Council").[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] Gretchen Robertson said that the restructure could lead to fragmentation while Neil Gillespie described local government as something "that was not simple."[[27]](#cite_note-28)

### Areas outside regional boundaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Areas outside regional boundaries")]

[![A map of New Zealand showing regional and district boundaries](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/NZ_Regional_Councils_and_Territorial_Authorities_2017.svg/500px-NZ_Regional_Councils_and_Territorial_Authorities_2017.svg.png)](/wiki/File:NZ_Regional_Councils_and_Territorial_Authorities_2017.svg)

Regions (indicated by colour) displayed over territorial authorities

Some outlying islands are not included within regional boundaries. The [Chatham Islands](/wiki/Chatham_Islands "Chatham Islands") is not in a region, although [its council](/wiki/Chatham_Islands_Council "Chatham Islands Council") has some of the powers of a regional council under the [Resource Management Act 1991](/wiki/Resource_Management_Act_1991 "Resource Management Act 1991"). The [Kermadecs](/wiki/Kermadec_Islands "Kermadec Islands") and the [subantarctic islands](/wiki/New_Zealand_Subantarctic_Islands "New Zealand Subantarctic Islands") are inhabited only by a small number of [Department of Conservation](/wiki/Department_of_Conservation_(New_Zealand) "Department of Conservation (New Zealand)") staff and there is no regional council for these islands.[[31]](#cite_note-34)

### Governance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Governance")]

Regional councils are popularly elected every three years in accordance with the Local Electoral Act 2001.[[32]](#cite_note-35) Councils may use a [first-past-the-post](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting") or [single transferable vote](/wiki/Single_transferable_vote "Single transferable vote") system. The chairperson is selected by the elected council members.[[33]](#cite_note-36)

### Finances

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Finances")]

Regional councils are funded through [property rates](/wiki/Rates_(tax) "Rates (tax)"), [subsidies](/wiki/Subsidy "Subsidy") from central government, income from trading, and user charges for certain public services. Councils set their own levels of rates,[[34]](#cite_note-LGRA-37) though the mechanism for collecting it usually involves channelling through the territorial authority collection system.

## Predecessors of current structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Predecessors of current structure")]

### Auckland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Auckland")]

The Auckland Regional Council (now the [Auckland Council](/wiki/Auckland_Council "Auckland Council")) was preceded by the [Auckland Regional Authority](/wiki/Auckland_Regional_Council#Auckland_Regional_Authority,_1963–1989 "Auckland Regional Council") (ARA), which existed from 1963 to 1989.[[47]](#cite_note-50)

### Wellington

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Wellington")]

The Wellington Regional Council was first formed in 1980 from a merger of the Wellington Regional Planning Authority and the Wellington Regional Water Board.[[48]](#cite_note-51)

### United councils

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Regions_of_New_Zealand&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: United councils")]

In 1978, legislation was passed enabling the formation of regions that had *united councils*. Twenty regions were designated, excluding the Auckland and Wellington areas. For most of the country this was the first time there had been a regional level of government since the abolition of [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_New_Zealand "Provinces of New Zealand") in 1876.
Councillors were not elected directly – they were appointed from the various territorial local authorities (TLAs) within the region.

The only responsibilities mandated by the legislation were coordination of [civil defence](/wiki/Civil_defense "Civil defense") and development of a regional plan, although the constituent TLAs could agree on additional responsibilities at the point of formation of each united council. For example, in a number of cases the united council took responsibility for the allocation of revenue from regional petrol taxes.

The united councils were based in the facilities of the largest TLA in the region and largely dependent on the TLAs for resources. They were allowed to levy rates but in most cases had minimal operating budgets (below $100,000 per annum). The notable exception was Canterbury, where the united council had a number of responsibilities. Only one united council undertook any direct operational activity – a forestry project in Wanganui.[[4]](#cite_note-bush-5)
